title: The Old-Age Security Motive for Fertility: Evidence from the Extension of Social Pensions in Namibia
interpretation: DiD coefficient on Post x InitialNeeds in the fully-controlled period specification: a 1,000-rand larger pre-reform old-age income shortfall (i.e. less generous initial pension) is associated with a 17.3-percentage-point larger decline in the annual probability of birth after the reform relative to before, controlling for year FE, cluster FE, urbanization, wealth, access to services, cost of children, AIDS prevalence, under-5 mortality and maternal education. The paper interprets this as pensions (removing old-age support needs) reducing fertility; the magnitude implies about 0.65 fewer completed children in the 'high initial needs' vs 'low initial needs' groups.
spec: Table 5 (Period analysis - Birth rate), column 8: full set of controls (urban, wealth, access to services, cost of children, AIDS, under-5 mortality, maternal education), year FE + cluster FE, SEs clustered at ethnicity*region level
quote: Post*InitialNeeds ... column (8): -0.173 (0.043); Observations 141637; Clusters 35; R2 0.015. Text: 'When we additionally control for access to health, cost of children, AIDS, child mortality and maternal education, the DD coefficient remains stable. The magnitude corresponds to a reduction by 0.65 additional children in group with high initial needs compared to groups with low initial needs.'

title: What Explains Fertility? Evidence from Italian Pension Reforms
interpretation: Individuals affected by the pension reform (i.e., facing a discontinuous reduction in expected future pension benefits) have on average 0.0529 more post-reform children than unaffected individuals, controlling for husband/wife age fixed effects, education, geographical area, pre-reform fertility, and survey-year dummies. This is a 13% increase relative to the sample average of 0.4010 post-reform children among the affected group.
spec: Table 3, column (1): OLS 'Amato+Dini Reform' — regression of the number of post-reform children on the Reform dummy plus controls (husband/wife age fixed effects, husband/wife education, Center/South area, number of children up to 1993, survey-year dummies); estimation window of +/- 7 years of contributions around the reform thresholds, wives born 1955 or later
quote: According to these estimates, after controlling for all these covariates, the average number of children for treated couples is 0.0529 higher since the 1992 reform (signiﬁcant at the 10% level). ... The magnitude of the ﬁrst eﬀect should be compared to an average of 0.4010 post-reform children (i.e. it amounts to 13% higher fertility). [Table 3] Reform 0.0529* (0.0287) ... N 2684 ... R-squared 0.320

title: The Introduction of Bismarck's Social Security System and its Effects on Marriage and Fertility in Prussia
outcome_units: difference-in-differences in log(count) fertility measure, relative to 1881 base/1882 comparison year
interpretation: DD(D,F,1910,1882): the change in Group D's (pension-only) fertility measure between 1882 and 1910, relative to the same change for Group F (no insurance), from the just-identified 2SLS fertility model. Positive sign means pension-only coverage is associated with slightly HIGHER fertility relative to no coverage by 1910 (opposite of the hypothesized child-substitution channel), but the difference is not statistically significant (p=.22). Analogous DD's at earlier horizons are also small and mostly insignificant: 1890 DD=0 (p=.02, significant but a null/zero point estimate), 1895 DD=.005 (p=.36), 1900 DD=.002 (p=.73), 1905 DD=.007 (p=.51).
spec: Table 5 ('Differences in differences for the fertility model'), row 'D v. F' ('Pension alone versus no coverage'), 1910 column; based on the just-identified 2SLS model that treats infant mortality and marriage as endogenous (district and year fixed effects, year effects interacted with treatment-intensity groups A-G)
quote: Table 5: Differences in differences for the fertility model ... D v. F / Pension alone versus no coverage / 1890: .02 [p], 0 [DD] / 1895: .36, .005 / 1900: .73, .002 / 1905: .51, .007 / 1910: .22, .018. Notes: See the notes to Table 4. Based on a just-identified 2SLS model that treats infant mortality and marriage as endogenous. [Table 4 notes:] 'The first number in each cell is the p-value from the two-sided test of the null hypothesis that DD equals zero... The second number in each cell is the magnitude of DD.' Text: 'Comparing D to F, on the other hand, separates the effect of old-age insurance by focusing on a group that had only this insurance (D) and another that had no insurance at all (F). We find no statistically significant effect.' [stated for the marriage model; fertility DD for D v F are likewise mostly insignificant]. Abstract: 'The old age insurance by itself tended to reduce marriages, but the health and accident-insurance components had the opposite effect. For people exposed to all three pillars of social insurance, the two effects cancelled each other and the aggregate effect on fertility was muted.'

title: The fertility effects of public pension: Evidence from the new rural pension scheme in China
interpretation: NRPS participation is associated with 0.119 fewer children (DiD ATT), controlling for covariates and province dummies
spec: Table 2, model (3): DD regression of Number of children on NRPS x Post, with individual/family covariates and province dummies, county-clustered SEs
quote: As shown in model (3) and (4), the estimated marginal effect of NRPS is significantly 0.119 fewer children and 5.3% decrease in the probability of second child for rural women who had participated in pension program compared to those who had not, which are similar with the results in model (1) and (2). -- Table 2: 'Number of children (3)' row 'NRPS x Post' = -0.119, (0.037); R2 = 0.291; Observations = 6,930; Province dummies = Yes.

title: Pensions and Fertility: Microeconomic Evidence
interpretation: The pension reform (rural x post-1991 DiD interaction) reduced the annual probability of childbearing among women aged 15-44 by 1.2 percentage points, a 9.8% decline relative to the baseline childbearing probability, in the fully-controlled specification.
spec: Table 3, column 6 (DiD, women aged 15-44, full controls: year & region FE, individual, job, household, and regional/group covariates; SEs clustered at regional level)
quote: DID women aged 15-44 ... -0.012*** (0.003) ... in % to baseline ... -9.8. [Table 3] ... 'The highly signiﬁcant reform eﬀect in the full sample of women in childbearing age is around one percentage point irrespective of the chosen speciﬁcation. This is equivalent to a decline in the probability of childbearing by 7-10%.'

title: The Impact of Social Security on Fertility Willingness in OECD Countries
interpretation: A one-percentage-point increase in pension expenditure as a share of GDP is associated with a 0.050-unit decrease in the total fertility rate, controlling for country and year fixed effects plus a battery of economic/demographic controls (GDP per capita, urbanization, medical spending, education spending, unemployment, household income, female labor participation, marriage rate, divorce rate).
spec: Table 4 ('Effect of pension expenditure on fertility'), column (1), contemporaneous pension expenditure, two-way (country + year) fixed-effects model
quote: Pension expenditure −0.050*** (−5.98) ... Both columns (1) and (2) of the regression results show that pension expenditure has a significant negative correlation with fertility, which is consistent with the benchmark regression results. ... Number of observation 809 ... R2 0.345
